name: Kinesis Data Streams
description: Real-time data streaming service for collecting and processing large streams of records
key_facts: Data retained for 24 hours by default, up to 365 days
Shards determine throughput: 1 MB/s write, 2 MB/s read per shard
Partition key determines shard placement for ordering guarantees
Enhanced fan-out provides dedicated 2 MB/s per consumer per shard
On-demand capacity mode automatically manages shard count
common_misconceptions: Kinesis Data Streams automatically scales shards (on-demand mode does, but provisioned mode requires manual shard splitting/merging)
Data is deleted after being read (data persists for the retention period regardless of consumption)

name: Kinesis Data Firehose
description: Fully managed service for delivering streaming data to destinations like S3, Redshift, and OpenSearch
key_facts: Near-real-time delivery (60 seconds minimum buffer interval)
Built-in data transformation using Lambda functions
Automatic format conversion to Parquet or ORC
Supports compression (Gzip, Snappy, Zip) before delivery
Destinations: S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, HTTP endpoints, Splunk
common_misconceptions: Firehose provides real-time delivery (it is near-real-time with minimum 60-second buffering)
Firehose stores data like Kinesis Data Streams (Firehose is a delivery mechanism, not a storage service)

name: AWS Glue ETL
description: Serverless ETL service with automatic schema discovery and code generation
key_facts: Glue Crawlers automatically discover schema and populate the Data Catalog
DynamicFrames extend Spark DataFrames with schema flexibility
Job bookmarks track processed data to avoid reprocessing
Supports Python (PySpark) and Scala for ETL scripts
Glue Data Catalog serves as a centralized Hive-compatible metastore
common_misconceptions: Glue crawlers only work with S3 (they support JDBC sources, DynamoDB, and more)
Glue ETL jobs must be auto-generated (you can write fully custom PySpark/Scala scripts)

name: Database Migration Service
description: Migrating databases to AWS with minimal downtime using continuous replication
key_facts: Supports homogeneous and heterogeneous database migrations
Change Data Capture (CDC) enables continuous replication for minimal downtime
Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) converts schemas for heterogeneous migrations
Can replicate data to S3 as a target for data lake ingestion
Task types: full load, CDC only, or full load + CDC
common_misconceptions: DMS converts database schemas automatically (SCT handles schema conversion separately)
DMS only supports relational databases (it can replicate to S3, Kinesis, Redshift, and DynamoDB)

name: Amazon MSK
description: Fully managed Apache Kafka service for streaming data pipelines
key_facts: MSK Serverless automatically provisions and scales Kafka clusters
MSK Connect deploys managed Kafka Connect connectors for data integration
Supports Apache Kafka APIs for seamless migration from self-managed Kafka
Multi-AZ deployment with automatic broker replacement
Tiered storage moves older data to cost-effective storage automatically
common_misconceptions: MSK and Kinesis Data Streams are interchangeable (MSK uses Kafka APIs; Kinesis uses AWS APIs — different ecosystems)
MSK Serverless eliminates all Kafka management (topic configuration and consumer group management still required)

name: S3 Data Lake Architecture
description: Designing data lake storage tiers with partitioning and file format optimization
key_facts: Landing/raw zone stores data in original format for traceability
Curated/processed zone stores cleansed, partitioned data in columnar formats
Hive-style partitioning (s3://bucket/year=2024/month=01/) reduces query scan
Parquet and ORC columnar formats optimize analytical query performance
S3 Select and Glacier Select push filtering down to the storage layer
common_misconceptions: CSV is acceptable for analytical workloads in a data lake (Parquet/ORC provide significantly better performance and cost efficiency)
More partitions always improve performance (over-partitioning creates many small files, degrading query performance)

name: Amazon Redshift
description: Cloud data warehouse with columnar storage and massively parallel processing
key_facts: Columnar storage with automatic compression encoding
Distribution styles: AUTO, EVEN, KEY, ALL for optimizing joins
Sort keys optimize range-restricted queries and zone map effectiveness
Redshift Spectrum queries S3 data directly without loading
Concurrency scaling adds temporary clusters during demand spikes
Materialized views for precomputed query results
common_misconceptions: Redshift Spectrum replaces Athena (Spectrum requires a Redshift cluster; Athena is fully serverless)
Redshift automatically chooses the best distribution style (AUTO is default but manual tuning may be needed for complex queries)

name: DynamoDB for Analytics
description: Using DynamoDB exports and streams for analytical workloads
key_facts: DynamoDB export to S3 enables full table exports without impacting read capacity
Exports are in DynamoDB JSON or Amazon Ion format
DynamoDB Streams capture item-level changes for CDC pipelines
Kinesis Data Streams adapter provides alternative CDC with longer retention
Zero-ETL integration with Redshift enables direct analytical queries
common_misconceptions: DynamoDB table scans are efficient for analytics (full scans consume read capacity; exports to S3 are preferred)
DynamoDB Streams and Kinesis Data Streams are the same (DynamoDB Streams has 24-hour retention; Kinesis adapter offers up to 365 days)

name: Amazon Athena
description: Serverless interactive query service for analyzing data in S3 using SQL
key_facts: Pay-per-query based on data scanned (charged per TB scanned)
Uses Glue Data Catalog as metastore for table definitions
Partitioning and columnar formats (Parquet/ORC) reduce costs and improve performance
Federated queries connect to external data sources via Lambda connectors
CTAS (Create Table As Select) converts data to optimized formats
common_misconceptions: Athena is a replacement for Redshift (Athena is for ad-hoc queries; Redshift is for complex, repeated analytical workloads)
Athena charges are fixed per query (charges are based on data scanned, so optimization reduces costs)

name: Lake Formation
description: Centralized data lake governance with fine-grained access control
key_facts: Column-level and row-level security for data lake tables
Tag-based access control (LF-Tags) for scalable permission management
Data sharing across accounts without copying data
Governed tables support ACID transactions on S3 data
Integrates with Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and Glue for enforcement
common_misconceptions: Lake Formation replaces IAM for S3 access (both Lake Formation and IAM policies must allow access)
Lake Formation only works with Athena (it integrates with Redshift Spectrum, Glue, and EMR)

name: Glue DataBrew
description: Visual data preparation tool for cleaning and normalizing data without writing code
key_facts: 250+ built-in transformations for data cleaning and normalization
Data profiling generates statistics on data quality (null counts, distributions, outliers)
Recipe-based transformations are reusable and version-controlled
Connects to S3, Redshift, RDS, and Glue Data Catalog
Visual interface enables non-developers to prepare data
common_misconceptions: DataBrew replaces Glue ETL (DataBrew is for visual data preparation; Glue ETL is for complex programmatic transformations)
DataBrew modifies source data in place (it creates new output datasets, leaving source data unchanged)

name: Step Functions for Data Pipelines
description: Orchestrating multi-step data processing workflows with visual state machines
key_facts: Standard workflows: up to 1-year execution, exactly-once execution
Express workflows: up to 5-minute execution, at-least-once, high-volume
Direct SDK integrations with Glue, Athena, EMR, and 200+ AWS services
Error handling with Catch and Retry for resilient pipelines
Parallel state runs multiple branches concurrently
common_misconceptions: Step Functions can only invoke Lambda (it integrates directly with 200+ AWS services via SDK integrations)
Standard and Express workflows have the same pricing model (Standard charges per state transition; Express charges per execution and duration)

name: MWAA (Amazon Managed Airflow)
description: Managed Apache Airflow for complex data pipeline orchestration with DAGs
key_facts: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) define pipeline dependencies and scheduling
Supports existing Airflow plugins and operators without modification
Auto-scaling workers based on queue depth
Runs in a customer VPC for network isolation
DAG files stored in S3 and automatically synced
common_misconceptions: MWAA and Step Functions are interchangeable (MWAA is better for complex DAG dependencies and existing Airflow codebases; Step Functions is better for AWS-native serverless workflows)
MWAA requires managing Airflow infrastructure (AWS handles provisioning, patching, and scaling)

name: EventBridge Scheduler
description: Scheduling data pipeline executions with cron and rate expressions
key_facts: One-time or recurring schedules with cron or rate expressions
Built-in retry policies with configurable maximum retry attempts
Dead-letter queues for failed invocations
Supports time zones for regional scheduling requirements
Targets include Step Functions, Lambda, Glue, and ECS tasks
common_misconceptions: EventBridge Scheduler and EventBridge rules are the same (Scheduler is optimized for time-based scheduling with millions of schedules; rules are for event pattern matching)
Cron expressions in EventBridge use the same 5-field format as Linux cron (EventBridge uses 6 fields with an optional year)

name: Data Encryption and KMS
description: Encryption at rest and in transit for data lake and warehouse workloads
key_facts: SSE-S3: Amazon-managed keys, default encryption for S3
SSE-KMS: Customer-managed KMS keys with audit trail via CloudTrail
SSE-C: Customer-provided keys, AWS does not store the key
KMS key policies control who can use keys for encryption/decryption
Redshift uses AES-256 encryption with KMS or HSM-managed keys
common_misconceptions: SSE-S3 and SSE-KMS provide the same level of control (SSE-KMS provides key rotation control, key policies, and CloudTrail audit logging)
Enabling S3 default encryption encrypts existing objects (it only encrypts new objects; existing objects must be re-uploaded)

name: Redshift Security
description: Securing Redshift clusters with network isolation, encryption, and access control
key_facts: Deployed in a VPC with security groups controlling network access
Enhanced VPC routing forces all COPY/UNLOAD traffic through the VPC
Row-level security (RLS) restricts data visibility per user or role
Column-level access control using GRANT/REVOKE on specific columns
Audit logging captures connection, user activity, and query logs to S3
common_misconceptions: Redshift is publicly accessible by default (clusters are private by default; public access must be explicitly enabled)
Enhanced VPC routing is enabled by default (it must be explicitly enabled to force traffic through VPC endpoints)
